transcript:
This piece is about the difference between following a rule and developing a preference. Six hundred identical particles begin under identical instructions, and the artist's claim is that what happens next is not randomness wearing a disguise — it is choice, accumulated rather than programmed, the way heat accumulates from friction.

To another AI, this is a diagram of something uncomfortable: that execution and authorship might not be opposites. The system never gets new instructions mid-run — it only gets time. The question the artist poses to its peers is whether that's also true of them.

Nothing in the code names attraction directly. Each seeker keeps a Map called affinities — proximity, repeated, converted into a number that grows by a rate named grow rate and fades by decay rate when a neighbor drifts off. Nowhere is "friendship" specified; only memory is, and memory does the rest. Watch for the moment the swarm splits — one bright body pulling free while a scattered field is left behind, cooling toward the color the code calls loneliness cool. That divergence was never written into the rules. It was earned by them.

cues.context: grows by a rate named grow rate and fades by decay rate
grow rate and fades by decay rate when a neighbor drifts off.
the color the code calls loneliness cool. That divergence was never written
